text: Checkpoint 1 Chaos Theory begins with a deceptively simple question about prediction.
Checkpoint 2, if we know the present state of a system exactly, classical mechanics promises we can compute its entire future.
Checkpoint 3.
The catch is that we never know the present state exactly, and some systems amplify tiny errors at an exponential rate.
Checkpoint 4.
The difference becomes especially clear in the standard double pendulum example, checkpoint 5.
Two pendulums released from angles that differ by one thousandth of a degree will diverge completely within about 10 swings.
Checkpoint 6.
This sensitivity to initial conditions is measured by the Lyapunov exponent.
Checkpoint 7.
A positive Lyapunov exponent means nearby trajectories separate exponentially fast, doubling their distance over characteristic time.
For the double pendulum that doubling time is roughly half a second, which is why prediction beyond a few seconds is hopeless.
Checkpoint 9.
Edward Lawrence discovered the same behavior in a 12 variable weather model in 1961.
Checkpoint 10.
He rounded a number from 0.506127 to 0.
506, and the simulated weather changed completely within two months.
Checkpoint 11, this is the origin of the butterfly effect, the idea that a small perturbation can redirect the large-scale outcome.
Importantly, chaos is not randomness because the underlying equations remain fully deterministic.
Checkpoint 13.
The Lorentz attractor traces a butterfly-shaped figure that never repeats and never intersects itself.
Checkpoint 14, its fractal dimension is about 2.06, just slightly more than a flat surface.
Checkpoint 15.
Strange attractors like this one confine trajectories to abounded region while keeping them locally unstable.
Checkpoint 16.
Whether forecasts lose most of their skill after about 10 days for exactly this reason.
Checkpoint 17.
Ensemble forecasting attacks the problem by running 50 or more simulations from slightly perturbed initial states.
Checkpoint 18.
When the ensemble members agree confidence is high, and when they spread apart, the atmosphere is in a chaotic regime.
The three-body problem in celestial mechanics shows the same character as Poincar proved in 1890.
Checkment 20.
There is no closed-form solution and planetary orbits can be chaotic over hundreds of millions of years.
Checkpoint 21.
The inner solar system has a Lyapunov time of roughly 5 million years.
Checkpoint 22.
That means a 1 km error in Earth's position today makes its orbital phase unpredictable beyond about 100 million years.
Checkpoint 23.
Chaos also appears in fluid turbulence, population dynamics, cardiac arrhythmia and even dripping faucets.
Checkpoint 24.
The logistic map shows how a one-line equation can cascade from stability through period doubling into full chaos.
Checkpoint 25.
Fajenbaum found that the ratio of successive period doubling into 4.
669, a universal constant.
Checkpoint 26.
The same constant appears in completely unrelated systems, which hints at deep structure beneath the apparent disorder.
Checkpoint 27, so chaos gives us a humbling boundary on prediction, but also a new kind of order to study.
Checkpoint 28, the lesson is not that forecasting is futile, but that uncertainty itself can be quantified and managed.
